game_name: Lufia II: Rise of the Sinistrals

danger_signals.visual_form: Sprite-based enemy entities visible on tile-based dungeon grid
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat requiring evasion or combat engagement

danger_signals.visual_form: Divine entity sprites (gods appearing as visual anomalies)
danger_signals.meaning: Ultimate danger/antagonistic force; existential threat to world state

danger_signals.visual_form: Tiles marked with spikes, obstacles, destructible barriers
danger_signals.meaning: Localized danger zones requiring tool-based interaction or avoidance

danger_signals.visual_form: Unique larger sprite, distinct color palette from standard monsters

safety_signals.visual_form: Interior tileset with NPCs, shops, churches visible
safety_signals.meaning: No random encounters; safe zone for trading and story progression

safety_signals.visual_form: Distinct religious architecture, altar or prayer visual markers
safety_signals.meaning: Healing and narrative safe-haven; faction-aligned spiritual location

safety_signals.visual_form: Clear visual shift from large-scale map to enclosed dungeon space
safety_signals.meaning: Entering structured, predictable environment; monsters appear on map rather than random encounters

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Each Sinistral appears as unique boss sprite with distinct visual design
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Sealed towers, dark fortresses, apocalyptic dungeon aesthetics

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Hero party members (Maxim, Tia, Guy, Dekar, Lexis, Artea, Selan); weapons and armor visual identity
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Towns (Elcid, Bound Kingdom, Parc), churches, human settlements, crafted dungeons

faction_indicators.color_identity: Seven elemental types: Neutral, Light (bright), Wind (cyan/blue), Water (blue), Dark (purple), Fire (red/orange), Soil (brown/green)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Distinct creature sprites for each element; visual form reflects elemental nature
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Evolution forms up to form M (5 stages per monster); visual complexity increases with growth
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Specific world locations where each Capsule Monster spawns; element-aligned dungeon aesthetics

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Normal towns, accessible dungeons, overworld appears stable and habitable
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: NPCs conduct normal routines; monsters are isolated threats rather than world-ending force

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Sealed towers appear on map; dark fortresses become accessible; ominous architecture introduces divine/apocalyptic aesthetic
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Hero party gathers; character recruitment escalates; stronger enemies appear in dungeons

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Boss encounters with Sinistrals; unique sprite designs for each god; increasingly complex dungeon layouts
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Party members gain new IP skills and abilities; equipment upgrades reflect power scaling; story-critical battles interrupt exploration

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: 99-floor randomly-generated dungeon with distinct visual theme; separate from main world; treasure chest variants (red/blue) signal item permanence
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Party stripped to Level 0 state; roguelike progression mechanics; permanent death risk creates high-stress environment

environmental_storytelling.example: Human settlements use warm interior tilesets; sealed towers and dark fortresses use oppressive, cold visual design; contrast communicates cultural/spiritual conflict
Main dungeons are puzzle-heavy (hundreds throughout game); Ancient Cave uses roguelike aesthetic; puzzle complexity signals both mechanical challenge and story importance
Weapons and armor are collectible visual markers; character customization reflects player agency and power progression; IP skill attachment to gear creates tactical narrative
Captured Capsule Monsters can be displayed in Forfeit Island; visual evolution stages (up to form M) communicate growth/power without narrative dialogue
Progression from walking → boat (submarine modification) → blimp communicates technological advancement and expanding world access
Ancient Cave uses red chests (temporary loot) vs blue chests (permanent items); visual distinction encodes item permanence rules without UI explanation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Appear stationary on dungeon tiles; only move in response to player movement (turn-based dungeon system); can be avoided without combat
Engage in turn-based battle; sprite animates during attacks; visual feedback through damage numbers and status effects
Unique sprite design signals story importance; often positioned in dungeon endpoints; surrounded by puzzle-gated access
Distinct attack animations; potential multiple-phase combat with visual state changes; larger sprite footprint than standard enemies

entity_behavioral_tells.visual_manifestation: Sealed towers appear on world map; divine presence implied through architecture rather than direct appearance

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Boss-tier combat sprites; unique visual designs for each of the seven; world state visibly shifts around encounters
Small sprite size; weak combat contribution; distinct elemental coloration visible even at low level
Sprite increases in visual complexity; evolves through 5 visible forms; form M represents ultimate evolution; stronger particle effects during attacks
Standard-sized sprites; basic equipment appearance; limited spell/IP skill animations
Equipped with late-game armor/weapons; more elaborate sprite animations; diverse IP skill visual effects; team coordination visible in multi-character attacks

notes: Lufia II communicates world-state primarily through dungeon architecture, NPC positioning, and sprite-based visual design. The game lacks explicit UI-based faction reputation or dynamic environmental degradation; instead, it uses static architectural contrast between human settlements and sealed/dark fortresses to communicate the human-Sinistral conflict. The turn-based dungeon system (monsters stationary until player acts) creates a low-pressure exploration state, contrasting sharply with randomized overworld encounters—this mechanical duality is reinforced visually through tileset shifts. The Ancient Cave's roguelike structure is communicated through treasure chest color-coding and roguelike aesthetic rather than narrative exposition. Capsule Monster evolution is the game's primary visual progression mechanic outside combat, with five distinct evolution stages visible to the player. Puzzle density (hundreds throughout dungeons) serves as both mechanical and narrative device, signaling dungeons' importance through environmental complexity.
